episode_title: The Craft of Diplomacy: Trust, Negotiation, and U.S. Foreign Policy
host: Dori Pham (UC Riverside School of Public Policy)
summary: Ambassador Jeffrey DeLantis discusses his diplomatic career spanning decades, with particular focus on U.S.-Cuba relations and multilateral diplomacy at the United Nations. He emphasizes that building trust with adversaries is fundamental to successful diplomacy, as evidenced by the 2015 normalization of U.S.-Cuba relations after 50+ years of estrangement. DeLantis reflects on the Cuban Missile Crisis, the Obama administration's JCPOA nuclear agreement with Iran, and current U.S.-Iran tensions, arguing throughout that diplomatic engagement and negotiation are preferable to military escalation. He concludes with advice for aspiring diplomats, highlighting emotional intelligence, political instincts, and current awareness as critical skills.
claims_to_note: Cuba's economy contracted by 35 percent during the 'special period' following Soviet Union's collapse (1991-1993)
Ambassador attributes Iran conflict escalation to abandonment of JCPOA negotiations and diplomatic channels
Claims that majority of American people do not support current U.S.-Iran military conflict
States that oil and energy prices have risen as a result of Iran conflict
policy_relevance: U.S.-Cuba diplomatic normalization (2014-2015) under Obama administration
JCPOA (Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action) nuclear agreement with Iran negotiated under Obama administration; withdrawn by Trump administration in 2017
U.S. diplomatic corps staffing losses mentioned under current administration
Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) referenced as near-nuclear exchange crisis
Kennedy administration speech at American University (June 10, 1963) on U.S.-Soviet relations
Current U.S.-Israel military strikes on Iran and regional escalation
United Nations Security Council and General Assembly negotiations
National Security Council role in major foreign policy decisions
State Department and assistant secretary positions in foreign policy decision-making
key_quotes: When the secret negotiations began in the latter part of the first half of 2013, it was just massive distrust of American intentions. And so to build that trust, to begin to have this sort of relationship that you need with interlockers, even adversaries to make any progress, this took a lot of time.

I always think it's better to try and resolve things diplomatically. We did see that achieved during the Obama administration. The Trump administration came in and tore up the agreement, and we're now at war.

I actually think that emotional intelligence probably is the most important. If you have a decent relationship with your interlocutor, adversary or not, there's a certain level of professionalism and trust. I think that helps a lot.
